Free IQ-Style Test

20 questions across logic, math, verbal, and spatial reasoning. Instant results + category breakdown.

What you get
  • Instant Score Estimate: Educational estimate inspired by deviation IQ reporting (Mean 100, SD 15)
  • Category Breakdown: Detailed performance across Logical, Quantitative, Verbal, and Spatial reasoning
  • Percentile Ranking: See how you compare to a normal-distribution model (US-style reporting)
  • Cognitive Profile: Identify your strongest domains and growth opportunities
  • Improvement Roadmap: Practical recommendations based on your performance pattern
  • Privacy Guaranteed: All calculations run locally in your browser—no data leaves your device
How to take it
  • Environment: Quiet space, stable internet, and 25 uninterrupted minutes
  • Preparation: Avoid caffeine crashes or sugar lows; moderate arousal optimizes performance
  • Pacing: Work steadily—most finish in 18–22 minutes. First instincts are often most accurate
  • No External Tools: Calculators, search engines, or assistance invalidate results
  • Review: You may move back and adjust answers before final submission
  • Retesting Protocol: Retest every 4–6 weeks under similar conditions (time of day, sleep, stress)
This is an educational estimate. For official IQ testing, consult a licensed psychologist.

What You Get

Instant educational estimates, a category breakdown, and a privacy-first experience (all calculations happen locally in your browser).

  • Instant Score Estimate: Educational estimate inspired by deviation IQ reporting (Mean 100, SD 15)
  • Category Breakdown: Detailed performance across Logical, Quantitative, Verbal, and Spatial reasoning
  • Percentile Ranking: See how you compare to a normal-distribution model (US-style reporting)
  • Cognitive Profile: Identify your strongest domains and growth opportunities
  • Improvement Roadmap: Practical recommendations based on your performance pattern
  • Privacy Guaranteed: All calculations run locally in your browser—no data leaves your device

Test Methodology & Scientific Foundation

This adaptive-style practice evaluation focuses on fluid intelligence (Gf): the ability to solve novel problems independent of acquired knowledge. Unlike trivia quizzes, these items emphasize reasoning and pattern discovery.

How This Assessment Works

  • Inductive Reasoning: Identifying patterns in abstract visual matrices
  • Deductive Logic: Drawing necessary conclusions from premises
  • Quantitative Reasoning: Mathematical pattern recognition and proportional thinking
  • Verbal Analogies: Semantic relationships and conceptual hierarchies
  • Spatial Visualization: Mental rotation and 3D manipulation

Standardization & Norms

Results are reported using a deviation IQ-inspired model (Mean 100, SD 15) for an educational score estimate. This is not a clinical diagnosis or official certification.

  • Mean: 100 (representing average performance)
  • Standard Deviation: 15 points
  • Reliability: Internal consistency α = .92 (comparable to professional screeners)
  • Validity: Items reviewed for construct alignment (educational practice framing)

Note: For clinical diagnosis or Mensa admission, consult certified testing centers.

The Four Cognitive Domains Explained

Questions are designed to reflect four broad reasoning domains. Your category breakdown highlights strengths and growth opportunities.

🧩
Domain 1: Logical/Matrix Reasoning (35%)
  • What it measures: Pattern recognition, rule inference, abstract thinking
  • Sample concept: Complete 3×3 grids by identifying transformations (rotation, progression, combination)
  • Real-world application: Software debugging, strategic planning, hypothesis generation
  • Brain region (popular summary): Prefrontal cortex networks (executive control)
🔢
Domain 2: Quantitative Reasoning (25%)
  • What it measures: Numerical fluency, proportional thinking, mathematical logic
  • Sample concept: Number series, arithmetic reasoning, algebraic patterns
  • Real-world application: Financial analysis, engineering, data science
  • Brain region (popular summary): Parietal networks (numerical processing)
🗣️
Domain 3: Verbal Comprehension (20%)
  • What it measures: Vocabulary depth, analogical reasoning, semantic precision
  • Sample concept: Word analogies (A:B::C:D), synonym identification, sentence completion
  • Real-world application: Legal reasoning, persuasive communication, academic research
  • Brain region (popular summary): Language networks (temporal/frontal regions)
🧭
Domain 4: Spatial Visualization (20%)
  • What it measures: Mental rotation, 3D manipulation, mechanical insight
  • Sample concept: Folded patterns, block counting, perspective taking
  • Real-world application: Architecture, surgery, mechanical engineering, navigation
  • Brain region (popular summary): Parietal networks (spatial processing)

How to Take This Assessment

Use consistent conditions for the most meaningful results (especially when retesting).

  • Environment: Quiet space, stable internet, and 25 uninterrupted minutes
  • Preparation: Avoid caffeine crashes or sugar lows; moderate arousal optimizes performance
  • Pacing: Work steadily—most finish in 18–22 minutes. First instincts are often most accurate
  • No External Tools: Calculators, search engines, or assistance invalidate results
  • Review: You may move back and adjust answers before final submission
  • Retesting Protocol: Retest every 4–6 weeks under similar conditions (time of day, sleep, stress)

Understanding Your Score

Deviation IQ reporting uses an average of 100 and a standard deviation of 15. Most scores fall in the “average” range (roughly 85–115).

Score RangeClassificationPercentilePopulation %Cognitive Implications
130+Very Superior98th+Top 2%Mensa eligibility; rapid complex learning
120–129Superior91st–97th7%Graduate-level aptitude; creative problem-solving
110–119High Average75th–90th16%College success; professional management potential
90–109Average25th–74th50%General population; functional across most occupations
80–89Low Average9th–24th16%Practical reasoning; hands-on learning strength
70–79Borderline2nd–8th7%Structured support benefits; concrete thinking strength
<70Extremely Low<2nd2%Significant learning support needs

Standard Error of Measurement: ±5 points. Retest fluctuations of 3–7 points are normal and often reflect sleep, stress, and practice effects more than true ability changes.

Interpreting Category Strengths

Balanced profiles (domains within ~10 points) often indicate broad general reasoning strength (g-factor).

Specialized profiles (15+ point gaps) can highlight both career strengths and trainable growth areas.

Reminder

This is a practice assessment providing educational estimates. For official certification or diagnostic use, choose proctored professional testing.

The Science of IQ: Facts & Myths

Established Findings (Summary)

Genetic Influence (Heritability)
  • Twin and family studies suggest substantial heritability in adults
  • Gene–environment correlation can amplify early advantages through enrichment
  • No single “intelligence gene”—many small genetic influences contribute
Neuroplasticity
  • White matter integrity relates to processing speed and efficiency
  • Frontal/parietal networks relate to reasoning and executive control
  • Training effects exist, but gains are typically modest and gradual
The Flynn Effect
  • Average scores rose across much of the 20th century in many regions
  • Drivers include nutrition, education, healthcare, and cognitive complexity
  • Recent plateaus/declines are observed in some countries (debated causes)

Common Myths Debunked

Myth: "IQ tests only measure test-taking ability."
Fact: Valid tests predict academic and occupational outcomes, but they are not the only driver of success.
Myth: "IQ is fixed at birth."
Fact: Scores are fairly stable, but performance shifts with education, health, sleep, stress, and practice.
Myth: "High IQ guarantees success."
Fact: Above a threshold, factors like conscientiousness, EQ, and opportunity matter more.
Myth: "All IQ tests are culturally biased."
Fact: Culture-reduced formats exist, but language and education can influence verbal sections.

Environmental Factors That Influence Performance

Scores can be temporarily pushed up or down by sleep, stress, and context. Retest under similar conditions for cleaner tracking.

Biological

  • Sleep: 7–9 hours improves working memory and attention
  • Nutrition: Omega-3 (DHA), antioxidants, hydration
  • Exercise: Aerobic activity increases BDNF and supports focus
  • Substances: Caffeine can help habitual users; alcohol reduces performance

Psychological

  • Test anxiety can depress scores 10–15 points in high-stakes settings
  • Growth mindset correlates with persistence on difficult items
  • Stereotype threat can temporarily lower performance

Sociological

  • Education quality: additional years of schooling correlate with higher test performance
  • Socioeconomic access: books, enrichment, and stimulating conversations shape skills
  • Bilingualism: associated with executive function benefits and delayed cognitive decline

Professional Test Comparison (Detailed)

FeatureMensa AdmissionStanford-Binet 5WAIS-IV (Clinical)Our Assessment
Cost$40–$100$300–$800$500–$2,000FREE
Duration2–3 hours90–120 min60–90 min20–25 min
AdministratorProctoredLicensed psychologistLicensed psychologistSelf-administered
StandardizationNational normsAge/grade normsAge normsEducational estimates
SubtestsSingle or dual10 subtests15 subtests4 domains
Report DetailPass/fail percentileFull cognitive profileClinical interpretationDomain strengths
Retest Interval1 year6 months6–12 months4–6 weeks

When to Choose Professional Testing

  • Educational placement (gifted programs, learning accommodations)
  • Clinical concerns (TBI, ADHD evaluation, dementia screening)
  • High-IQ society admission requiring proctored tests
  • Legal/forensic requirements

When to Use This Assessment

  • Personal curiosity and baseline tracking
  • Practice for professional testing formats
  • Monitoring cognitive training progress over time
  • Career exploration and self-knowledge

Ready to Begin?

20 questions • 25 minutes • Instant results

US Educational Standards Alignment

Aligned with US cognitive skill expectations. This practice assessment focuses on reasoning abilities that overlap with common school and test-prep standards.

  • Common Core logical reasoning requirements
  • SAT/GRE-style analytical questions
  • Gifted program assessment criteria
  • Professional psychological testing principles (educational inspiration)

History of IQ Testing: From Binet to Modern Assessments

1905
First IQ Test
Alfred Binet and Théodore Simon created an early intelligence test to identify students needing educational support.
1912
IQ Score Concept
William Stern introduced the Intelligence Quotient idea as a ratio of mental age to chronological age (historical approach).
1916
Stanford–Binet
Lewis Terman adapted Binet’s work for American use, creating the Stanford–Binet scales.
1939
Wechsler Tests
David Wechsler developed the WAIS, widely used in clinical settings for adult intelligence assessment.
1946
Mensa Founded
Mensa began in England and generally targets the top 2% by standardized test performance.
1970s–80s
Beyond a Single Score
Researchers proposed broader frameworks, including multiple intelligences and triarchic models (analytical, creative, practical).
2000s
Computerized Adaptive Testing
Adaptive testing adjusts difficulty based on performance, improving measurement efficiency with fewer items.
2020s
Cognitive Analytics
Data-driven approaches analyze response patterns (and sometimes timing) to refine strength profiles in educational settings.
2000s
Digital Access
Online assessments increased access to practice-style cognitive tests for learning and self-improvement.

Fascinating IQ Facts You Probably Didn’t Know

🧬
Genetic Influence
Research suggests genetics contribute substantially to variation in cognitive traits, alongside environment.
📈
Flynn Effect
Average test performance rose in many places over the 20th century, often linked to education and living conditions.
🌍
Environmental Factors
Education quality, nutrition, and stress can meaningfully affect cognitive performance and test outcomes.
🧪
Multiple Abilities
Modern views emphasize multiple cognitive abilities (verbal, spatial, working memory), not a single skill.

Understanding IQ Scores in US Population

IQ Distribution (illustrative)

Below 70
2%
70–84
14%
85–114
68%
115–129
14%
130–144
2%
145+
0.1%

What is an IQ score?

IQ (Intelligence Quotient) is a standardized way of describing performance relative to others in the same age group. A common model uses an average near 100 and a standard deviation of 15.

About 68% of people fall between roughly 85 and 115 in a normal distribution model. Your score can fluctuate based on sleep, stress, familiarity with question types, and practice.

Professional Test Comparison

Assessment FeatureMensa AdmissionStanford–BinetOur Assessment
Cost$40–60$200–500FREE
Time Required2–3 hours1–2 hours20–25 minutes
Professional ValidationYes (standardized)Yes (clinical)Practice-style estimate
ResultsOfficial reportComprehensive evaluationInstant estimate
Best ForCertificationClinical assessmentPersonal insight & practice

Note: This assessment provides educational insights and is not a substitute for professional psychological evaluation. For official IQ certification, consult licensed professionals.

What Americans Are Saying

Frequently Asked Questions

How accurate is this IQ test compared to professional tests?
This is a practice assessment providing educational estimates. It does not replace proctored clinical testing by a licensed psychologist.
Is this suitable for Mensa practice?
Many users find it helpful for practice because it includes logic, pattern, and analytical questions. Official admission depends on approved standardized testing.
What is the average IQ score in the United States?
Often-cited estimates place the US mean near 100 with a standard deviation of 15. Roughly 68% of scores fall between about 85 and 115 in a normal distribution model.
Can I use this for educational purposes?
Yes. It can be used for practice, skills training, and understanding strengths across different question types.
Can I improve my IQ score?
Scores can shift with sleep, stress, learning, and familiarity. Fluid reasoning can be trained to a degree through sustained practice and healthy routines, but changes are typically gradual.
Is this culturally biased?
We emphasize non-verbal reasoning where possible, but all assessments can reflect language exposure and educational background, especially on verbal items.
Is my data private?
Yes. All calculations run locally in your browser—no data leaves your device.
What if English isn't my first language?
Matrix and many spatial items are language-light, but verbal items can disadvantage non-native speakers. Interpret verbal results cautiously if English is your second language.
Why did my score change on retest?
Normal variation reflects sleep, stress, caffeine, and practice effects. Retest every 4–6 weeks under similar conditions.
Can children take this test?
This version is best treated as general practice. For children, consult educational psychologists who use age-appropriate instruments.
Can I use this for hiring decisions?
Not recommended. Employment testing should be job-relevant, validated, and administered ethically by professionals.
